abstract:
Training large transformer models is one of the most important computational
challenges of modern AI. In this paper, we show how to significantly accelerate
training of large transformer models by reducing activation recomputation.
Activation recomputation is commonly used to work around memory capacity
constraints. Rather than storing activations for backpropagation, they are
traditionally recomputed, which saves memory but adds redundant compute. In
this work, we show most of this redundant compute is unnecessary because we can
reduce memory consumption sufficiently without it. We present two novel yet
very simple techniques: sequence parallelism and selective activation
recomputation. In conjunction with tensor parallelism, these techniques almost
eliminate the need to recompute activations. We evaluate our approach on
language models up to one trillion parameters in scale and show that our method
reduces activation memory by 5x, while reducing execution time overhead from
activation recomputation by over 90%. For example, when training a 530B
parameter GPT-3 style model on 2240 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, we achieve a Model Flops
Utilization of 54.2%, which is 29% faster than the 42.1% we achieve using
recomputation. Our implementation will be available in both Megatron-LM and
NeMo-Megatron.

keyInsights: A hybrid parallelism approach, combining tensor and sequence parallelism, efficiently distributes activation memory for both matrix multiplication and non-matrix multiplication operations.
Targeted activation recomputation on specific high-memory but low-compute components, such as the attention block, significantly reduces computational overhead compared to full layer recomputation.
Optimizing memory and computation based on the distinct characteristics of different transformer components leads to better overall training efficiency and scalability.
originalProblem: Training large transformer models faces significant memory constraints, primarily due to the large memory footprint of activations stored for the backward pass.
Traditional solutions like full activation recomputation substantially increase computation time (30-40% overhead).
Existing tensor parallelism techniques do not adequately address activation memory for non-matrix-multiplication operations like layer normalization and dropout.
results: The combined techniques reduced activation memory by approximately 5x compared to using tensor parallelism alone.
Recomputation execution time overhead was reduced from over 30% (full recomputation) to just 3%.
Training throughput improved by 29-32% compared to full activation recomputation, and achieved 54.2% Model Flops Utilization (MFU) for a 530B parameter model.
solution: Sequence parallelism partitions the sequence dimension across devices for operations that are not tensor-parallel (e.g., layer normalization, dropout), reducing their activation memory footprint.
Selective activation recomputation recomputes only specific, memory-intensive but computationally less expensive parts of the attention block (e.g., attention pattern) during the backward pass.
These two complementary approaches are integrated into a hybrid parallelism strategy alongside existing tensor and pipeline parallelism.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from NVIDIA developed sequence parallelism and selective activation recomputation to mitigate activation memory bottlenecks and enhance training throughput for large transformer models. These methods achieved approximately 5x reduction in activation memory and a 29-32% improvement in training throughput while reducing recomputation overhead to just 3%.

title: Reducing Activation Recomputation in Large Transformer Models

abstract:
Large deep learning models offer significant accuracy gains, but training
billions to trillions of parameters is challenging. Existing solutions such as
data and model parallelisms exhibit fundamental limitations to fit these models
into limited device memory, while obtaining computation, communication and
development efficiency. We develop a novel solution, Zero Redundancy Optimizer
(ZeRO), to optimize memory, vastly improving training speed while increasing
the model size that can be efficiently trained. ZeRO eliminates memory
redundancies in data- and model-parallel training while retaining low
communication volume and high computational granularity, allowing us to scale
the model size proportional to the number of devices with sustained high
efficiency. Our analysis on memory requirements and communication volume
demonstrates: ZeRO has the potential to scale beyond 1 Trillion parameters
using today's hardware.
  We implement and evaluate ZeRO: it trains large models of over 100B parameter
with super-linear speedup on 400 GPUs, achieving throughput of 15 Petaflops.
This represents an 8x increase in model size and 10x increase in achievable
performance over state-of-the-art. In terms of usability, ZeRO can train large
models of up to 13B parameters (e.g., larger than Megatron GPT 8.3B and T5 11B)
without requiring model parallelism which is harder for scientists to apply.
Last but not the least, researchers have used the system breakthroughs of ZeRO
to create the world's largest language model (Turing-NLG, 17B parameters) with
record breaking accuracy.

keyInsights: Replicating model states (optimizer states, gradients, parameters) across devices in data parallelism is the primary memory bottleneck for large models.
Partitioning these model states across data-parallel devices, rather than replicating them, allows for linear memory reduction while maintaining high computational efficiency.
Optimizing 'residual states' such as activations and temporary buffers, and mitigating memory fragmentation, are crucial for further memory gains and overall stability in large-scale training.
originalProblem: GPU memory limitations prevent training deep learning models larger than approximately 1.4 billion parameters using standard data parallelism.

Existing parallelization strategies (model, pipeline) introduce significant trade-offs in computational efficiency, communication overhead, or ease of use when scaling to models beyond tens of billions of parameters.

The inability to efficiently train models with hundreds of billions to trillions of parameters hinders further advancements in AI, especially in fields like NLP where model size correlates with performance.
results: ZeRO-100B enabled efficient training of models with up to 170 billion parameters, an 8x increase over the previously efficient maximum of 40 billion parameters.
Achieved a 10x improvement in training speed over baseline Megatron-LM for similar model sizes, with sustained throughput exceeding 15 Petaflops and observed super-linear speedup.
The system directly powered Microsoft's 17-billion-parameter Turing-NLG, which achieved a new state-of-the-art Webtext-103 perplexity of 10.21.
solution: ZeRO-DP partitions memory-intensive model states (optimizer states, gradients, and parameters) across data-parallel devices, rather than replicating them, to reduce memory consumption linearly with the number of devices.

ZeRO-R introduces optimizations for 'residual states' by partitioning activations for model parallelism, using constant-size buffers for collective operations, and implementing memory defragmentation.

The approach strategically manages communication overhead, ensuring that memory savings do not come at the expense of computational efficiency, often leading to performance comparable to or better than standard data parallelism.
paper_summary.summary: ZeRO, developed by Microsoft, is a memory optimization strategy implemented within the DeepSpeed library that allows for efficient training of deep learning models with up to 170 billion parameters. This system optimizes memory use by partitioning model and residual states across devices, enabling unprecedented model scales while achieving high training throughput and powering models like Turing-NLG.

title: ZeRO: Memory Optimizations Toward Training Trillion Parameter Models

abstract:
Fine-tuning large pre-trained LLMs generally demands extensive GPU memory.
Traditional first-order optimizers like SGD encounter substantial difficulties
due to increased memory requirements from storing activations and gradients
during both the forward and backward phases as the model size expands.
Alternatively, zeroth-order (ZO) techniques can compute gradients using just
forward operations, eliminating the need to store activations. Furthermore, by
leveraging CPU capabilities, it's feasible to enhance both the memory and
processing power available to a single GPU. We propose a novel framework, ZO2
(Zeroth-Order Offloading), for efficient zeroth-order fine-tuning of LLMs with
only limited GPU memory. Our framework dynamically shifts model parameters
between the CPU and GPU as required, optimizing computation flow and maximizing
GPU usage by minimizing downtime. This integration of parameter adjustments
with ZO's double forward operations reduces unnecessary data movement,
enhancing the fine-tuning efficacy. Additionally, our framework supports an
innovative low-bit precision approach in AMP mode to streamline data exchanges
between the CPU and GPU. Employing this approach allows us to fine-tune
extraordinarily large models, such as the OPT-175B with more than 175 billion
parameters, on a mere 18GB GPU--achievements beyond the reach of traditional
methods. Moreover, our framework achieves these results with almost no
additional time overhead and absolutely no accuracy loss compared to standard
zeroth-order methods. ZO2's code has been open-sourced in
this https URL

organization_info.name: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Efficient parameter updates before dual forward passes reduce data transfer overhead
Dynamic scheduling of CPU-GPU communication and computation tasks maximizes throughput
Low-bit compression in AMP mode further reduces memory requirements without sacrificing accuracy
originalProblem: Fine-tuning extremely large language models requires substantial GPU memory, limiting accessibility
Existing CPU offloading and memory optimization techniques often sacrifice training speed or accuracy
results: Enables fine-tuning of OPT-175B on a single 18GB GPU
Maintains comparable throughput to baseline MeZO method
Achieves equivalent accuracy across NLP benchmarks while reducing memory usage
Demonstrates effectiveness of compression techniques (FP8, BF16, FP16) for communication-bound scenarios
solution: Combines zeroth-order optimization with strategic CPU offloading of transformer blocks
Implements dynamic scheduling and reusable memory blocks to maximize GPU utilization
Uses RNG state management to ensure consistency between parameter updates
paper_summary.summary: KAUST researchers develop ZO2, a framework that enables fine-tuning of large language models on limited GPU memory through CPU offloading and zeroth-order optimization, allowing models like OPT-175B to be fine-tuned on a single 18GB GPU while maintaining accuracy comparable to traditional methods.

title: ZO2: Scalable Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning for Extremely Large Language  Models with Limited GPU Memory

abstract:
We present MegaScale-MoE, a production system tailored for the efficient training of large-scale mixture-of-experts (MoE) models. MoE emerges as a promising architecture to scale large language models (LLMs) to unprecedented sizes, thereby enhancing model performance. However, existing MoE training systems experience a degradation in training efficiency, exacerbated by the escalating scale of MoE models and the continuous evolution of hardware.
Recognizing the pivotal role of efficient communication in enhancing MoE training, MegaScale-MoE customizes communication-efficient parallelism strategies for attention and FFNs in each MoE layer and adopts a holistic approach to overlap communication with computation at both inter- and intra-operator levels. Additionally, MegaScale-MoE applies communication compression with adjusted communication patterns to lower precision, further improving training efficiency. When training a 352B MoE model on 1,440 NVIDIA Hopper GPUs, MegaScale-MoE achieves a training throughput of 1.41M tokens/s, improving the efficiency by 1.88$\times$ compared to Megatron-LM. We share our operational experience in accelerating MoE training and hope that by offering our insights in system design, this work will motivate future research in MoE systems.

keyInsights: Confining MoE layers to single nodes effectively minimizes costly cross-node communication by leveraging high-bandwidth intra-node NVLink.

Customizing parallelism, such as using Sequence Parallelism for attention and Expert Parallelism for FFNs, significantly improves Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU) for MoE models compared to standard approaches.

Comprehensive communication-computation overlap at both macro and micro levels, especially fusing dependent communication and computation operations, is critical for hiding communication latency and boosting training throughput.
originalProblem: Large-scale MoE models incur significant communication overhead (e.g., 43.6% of forward pass time) due to increased parameter sizes requiring more GPUs and specialized all-to-all operations for token dispatch and aggregation.
The widening gap between GPU computational power and network bandwidth makes communication a dominant performance bottleneck, limiting the scalability of MoE models.
Existing distributed training frameworks lack comprehensive optimizations for the unique communication patterns and requirements of MoE models at production scale.
results: MegaScale-MoE demonstrated a 1.88x throughput improvement over Megatron-LM, achieving 1.41M tokens/s when training a 352B MoE model on 1,440 NVIDIA Hopper GPUs.

Ablation studies confirmed that customized parallelism strategies (SP+EP) led to 14.9%-32.9% higher MFU, and intra-operator overlap reduced combined operation time by 1.2-4.7x, cutting overall iteration time by 7.1%-12.9%.

The system is deployed in ByteDance's data centers, where it has trained models with trillions of parameters on over 10,000 GPUs for months with stable convergence (BF16 and FP8), saving millions of GPU hours.
solution: Developed MegaScale-MoE, a production system that confines each MoE layer within a single node to maximize the use of high-bandwidth intra-node NVLink.

Implemented communication-efficient parallelism strategies, including Sequence Parallelism (SP) for attention and Expert Parallelism (EP) for FFNs, augmented with adaptive communication patterns and custom CUDA operators.

Achieved comprehensive communication-computation overlap through holistic inter-operator scheduling (including Selective Activation Rematerialization) and fine-grained intra-operator fusion of communication and computation into single CUDA kernels.
paper_summary.summary: A production-grade system named MegaScale-MoE, developed by Peking University and ByteDance Seed, significantly improves the efficiency of large-scale Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model training by mitigating communication bottlenecks. It achieves a 1.88x speedup over Megatron-LM for a 352B MoE model on 1,440 NVIDIA Hopper GPUs and has saved millions of GPU hours in ByteDance's data centers.

title: MegaScale-MoE: Large-Scale Communication-Efficient Training of Mixture-of-Experts Models in Production

abstract:
Pipeline Parallelism (PP) serves as a crucial technique for training Large
Language Models (LLMs), owing to its capability to alleviate memory pressure
from model states with relatively low communication overhead. However, in
long-context scenarios, existing pipeline parallelism methods fail to address
the substantial activation memory pressure, primarily due to the peak memory
consumption resulting from the accumulation of activations across multiple
microbatches. Moreover, these approaches inevitably introduce considerable
pipeline bubbles, further hindering efficiency.
To tackle these challenges, we propose SlimPipe, a novel approach to
fine-grained pipeline parallelism that employs uniform sequence slicing coupled
with one-forward-one-backward (1F1B) schedule. It reduces the accumulated
activations from several microbatches to just one, which is split into several
slices. Although the slices are evenly partitioned, the computation cost is not
equal across slices due to causal attention. We develop a sophisticated
workload redistribution technique to address this load imbalance. SlimPipe
achieves (1) near-zero memory overhead and (2) minimal pipeline bubbles
simultaneously. The effectiveness of SlimPipe has been proven by thorough
testing with diverse model architectures, context window sizes, and
SlimPipe-specific configurations. For example, on the Llama 70B model, compared
to state-of-the-art methods, SlimPipe significantly boosts the Model FLOPs
Utilization (MFU) to up to $1.57\times$ for a context length of 512K. More
notably, for a context length of 2048K, it maintains over 45% utilization on
256 NVIDIA Hopper 80GB GPUs, while other approaches either suffer significant
performance drops or fail entirely due to memory constraints.

keyInsights: Activation memory consumption scales inversely with pipeline parallelism size
Workload imbalance from causal attention can be counteracted through redistribution
Vocabulary parallelism across pipeline devices reduces output layer bottlenecks
originalProblem: Traditional pipeline parallelism methods struggle with high activation memory overhead for long-context LLM training
Existing approaches introduce significant pipeline bubbles, reducing training efficiency
Memory demands of increasing context lengths exceed modern accelerator capabilities
results: Achieved 1.57x higher Model FLOPs Utilization compared to existing systems
Successfully trained Mixtral 8x7B with 4096K context length using activation offloading
Maintained high efficiency when scaling to 256/512 NVIDIA Hopper 80GB GPUs
Near-zero memory overhead while minimizing pipeline bubbles
solution: Uniform sequence slicing combined with one-forward-one-backward scheduling
Dynamic workload redistribution to balance computational loads across pipeline stages
Chunked KV cache management and early key-value exchange for efficient memory use
paper_summary.summary: Kuaishou Technology researchers present SlimPipe, a pipeline parallelism framework that enables training of large language models with ultra-long context lengths (up to 4096K tokens) while achieving 45.0% Model FLOPs Utilization on Llama 70B through uniform sequence slicing and workload redistribution techniques.

title: SlimPipe: Memory-Thrifty and Efficient Pipeline Parallelism for  Long-Context LLM Training

abstract: The high computational and memory requirements of large language model (LLM) inference make it feasible only with multiple high-end accelerators. Motivated by the emerging demand for latency-insensitive tasks with batched processing, this paper initiates the study of high-throughput LLM inference using limited resources, such as a single commodity GPU. We present FlexGen, a high-throughput generation engine for running LLMs with limited GPU memory. FlexGen can be flexibly configured under various hardware resource constraints by aggregating memory and computation from the GPU, CPU, and disk. By solving a linear programming problem, it searches for efficient patterns to store and access tensors. FlexGen further compresses the weights and the attention cache to 4 bits with negligible accuracy loss. These techniques enable FlexGen to have a larger space of batch size choices and thus significantly increase maximum throughput. As a result, when running OPT-175B on a single 16GB GPU, FlexGen achieves significantly higher throughput compared to state-of-the-art offloading systems, reaching a generation throughput of 1 token/s for the first time with an effective batch size of 144. On the HELM benchmark, FlexGen can benchmark a 30B model with a 16GB GPU on 7 representative sub-scenarios in 21 hours. The code is available at this https URL

keyInsights: Optimizing tensor placement and I/O scheduling across the entire memory hierarchy (GPU, CPU, disk) is crucial for achieving high throughput in generative LLM inference on limited hardware.
A "zig-zag block schedule" can dramatically improve I/O efficiency for generative inference by amortizing weight loading over multiple batches, outperforming traditional row-by-row methods.
Aggressive 4-bit quantization of both model weights and the KV cache can significantly reduce memory footprint and I/O while maintaining negligible accuracy loss for large LLMs.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) like OPT-175B require hundreds of gigabytes of memory, making their inference prohibitively expensive and typically necessitating multiple high-end GPUs.
Existing offloading systems for LLM inference are highly inefficient for generative tasks, often bottlenecked by very small batch sizes (e.g., 1-2), leading to unacceptably low throughput.
There is an emerging demand for high-throughput, latency-insensitive LLM applications (e.g., large-scale benchmarking, data wrangling) that current systems struggle to support on limited hardware.
results: Achieves a generation throughput of 0.69 tokens/s for OPT-175B on a single 16GB NVIDIA T4 GPU, increasing to 1.12 tokens/s with 4-bit compression, representing up to a 112x improvement over baselines like DeepSpeed ZeRO-Inference and Hugging Face Accelerate.
Enables the use of dramatically larger effective batch sizes (e.g., 144-256) compared to existing systems (e.g., 1-2), which is key to its high throughput.
Demonstrates that 4-bit quantization of both model weights and the KV cache introduces only minor accuracy or perplexity changes, validating its practical use for memory reduction.
solution: Develops a "zig-zag block schedule" with I/O-compute overlapping for generative inference, which amortizes expensive weight I/O over larger effective batch sizes by processing a block of batches layer-by-layer.
Implements a linear programming-based policy search to optimally place model weights, intermediate activations, and the Key-Value (KV) cache across the GPU-CPU-Disk memory hierarchy.

Integrates group-wise 4-bit quantization for both model weights and the KV cache to further reduce memory footprint, and explores delegating attention score computation to the CPU to reduce KV cache transfers.
paper_summary.summary: FlexGen enables high-throughput generative inference of large language models like OPT-175B on a single commodity GPU. It achieves this by proposing a novel memory offloading and scheduling system that optimally manages the GPU-CPU-Disk hierarchy and leverages 4-bit quantization, yielding up to 112x higher throughput compared to existing systems.

title: FlexGen: High-Throughput Generative Inference of Large Language Models with a Single GPU

abstract:
Large language models have led to state-of-the-art accuracies across a range
of tasks. However, training these models efficiently is challenging for two
reasons: a) GPU memory capacity is limited, making it impossible to fit large
models on even a multi-GPU server, and b) the number of compute operations
required to train these models can result in unrealistically long training
times. Consequently, new methods of model parallelism such as tensor and
pipeline parallelism have been proposed. Unfortunately, naive usage of these
methods leads to fundamental scaling issues at thousands of GPUs, e.g., due to
expensive cross-node communication or devices spending significant time waiting
on other devices to make progress.
  In this paper, we show how different types of parallelism methods (tensor,
pipeline, and data parallelism) can be composed to scale to thousands of GPUs
and models with trillions of parameters. We survey techniques for pipeline
parallelism and propose a novel interleaved pipeline parallelism schedule that
can improve throughput by 10+% with memory footprint comparable to existing
approaches. We quantitatively study the trade-offs between tensor, pipeline,
and data parallelism, and provide intuition as to how to configure distributed
training of a large model. Our approach allows us to perform training
iterations on a model with 1 trillion parameters at 502 petaFLOP/s on 3072 GPUs
with achieved per-GPU throughput of 52% of theoretical peak. Our code is open
sourced at https://github.com/nvidia/megatron-lm.

keyInsights: Optimal composition of Pipeline, Tensor, and Data Parallelism (PTD-P) is crucial for efficient large-scale language model training, enabling scaling to thousands of GPUs with high hardware utilization.
Tensor parallelism is best utilized within high-bandwidth multi-GPU nodes, while pipeline parallelism is more suitable for scaling across nodes due to its more favorable communication patterns.

Sophisticated scheduling (interleaved pipeline) and communication optimizations (scatter/gather) are essential to mitigate overheads like pipeline bubbles and inefficient cross-node data transfer, particularly at extreme scales.
originalProblem: Large language models exceed the memory capacity of individual GPUs, necessitating effective model parallelism across many devices.
Training times for models with hundreds of billions of parameters are prohibitively long, requiring efficient parallelization across thousands of accelerators.
Existing parallelism techniques (data, tensor, pipeline) face scaling limitations and inefficiencies when applied in isolation or simple combinations at extreme scales.
results: Achieved a record throughput of 502 petaFLOP/s for a 1-trillion-parameter GPT model on 3072 A100 GPUs, reaching 52% of the theoretical peak FLOP/s.
Reduced estimated training time for a 1-trillion-parameter model to approximately 84 days and a 175-billion-parameter GPT-3 model to 34 days on 1024 A100 GPUs.
Demonstrated up to 70% higher throughput compared to ZeRO-3 at larger scales for both 175-billion and 530-billion-parameter GPT models, showcasing the advantages of integrated model parallelism.
solution: The PTD-P strategy combines Pipeline Model Parallelism, Tensor Model Parallelism, and Data Parallelism, with tensor parallelism within nodes and pipeline parallelism across nodes.
A novel interleaved pipeline schedule is introduced to reduce pipeline bubble size, alongside scatter/gather communication optimizations for efficient inter-node data transfer.
System-level optimizations include data layout transformations, fused kernels for element-wise operations, and custom fused kernels for attention mechanisms to enhance computational efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at NVIDIA, Stanford University, and Microsoft Research developed the PTD-P strategy within Megatron-LM, enabling efficient training of large language models on GPU clusters. This approach successfully trained a 1-trillion-parameter GPT model on 3072 A100 GPUs, achieving 502 petaFLOP/s at 52% hardware utilization and outperforming ZeRO-3 by up to 70% at large scales.

title:
Efficient Large-Scale Language Model Training on GPU Clusters Using
  Megatron-LM

abstract:
The growth rate of the GPU memory capacity has not been able to keep up with
that of the size of large language models (LLMs), hindering the model training
process. In particular, activations -- the intermediate tensors produced during
forward propagation and reused in backward propagation -- dominate the GPU
memory use. This leads to high training overhead such as high weight update
cost due to the small micro-batch size. To address this challenge, we propose
SSDTrain, an adaptive activation offloading framework to high-capacity NVMe
SSDs. SSDTrain reduces GPU memory usage without impacting performance by fully
overlapping data transfers with computation. SSDTrain is compatible with
popular deep learning frameworks like PyTorch, Megatron, and DeepSpeed, and it
employs techniques such as tensor deduplication and forwarding to further
enhance efficiency. We extensively experimented with popular LLMs like GPT,
BERT, and T5. Results demonstrate that SSDTrain reduces 47% of the activation
peak memory usage. Meanwhile, SSDTrain perfectly overlaps the I/O with the
computation and incurs negligible overhead. Compared with keeping activations
in GPU memory and layerwise full recomputation, SSDTrain achieves the best
memory savings with negligible throughput loss. We further analyze how the
reduced activation memory use may be leveraged to increase throughput by
increasing micro-batch size and reducing pipeline parallelism bubbles.

keyInsights: NVMe SSDs offer a viable and high-capacity target for offloading activations, overcoming the limitations of host memory and enabling cost-effective scaling.
Full overlap of I/O and computation for activation offloading to SSDs is achievable, preventing performance degradation in LLM training.
Reducing activation memory allows for larger micro-batch sizes, which can further boost training throughput by improving GPU utilization and minimizing pipeline bubbles in distributed settings.
originalProblem: GPU memory capacity is not keeping pace with the exponential growth of Large Language Model (LLM) sizes, limiting training scale.
Activations, which are intermediate tensors, consume up to 80% of GPU memory during LLM training, making them the primary bottleneck.
Existing memory-saving techniques like gradient accumulation and activation checkpointing either underutilize hardware or still face scalability issues for future LLMs.
results: SSDTrain reduced peak activation memory by 28% to 47% across BERT, T5, and GPT models while maintaining the original training step time.

Performance modeling indicated that mainstream NVMe SSDs can sustain the write-intensive activation offloading workload for over two years, requiring no more than 12.1 GB/s PCIe write bandwidth per GPU.
The framework demonstrated superior memory efficiency and throughput compared to activation recomputation and no offloading strategies, enabling a doubling of batch size for the same memory budget.
solution: The SSDTrain framework adaptively offloads activations to high-capacity NVMe SSDs, utilizing GPUDirect Storage (GDS) for direct, high-bandwidth data transfers.
A novel tensor cache mechanism, managed through PyTorch hooks, intelligently decides which activations to offload and prefetch for the backward pass.
Asynchronous I/O operations are fully overlapped with GPU computation using dedicated thread pools to ensure performance neutrality.
paper_summary.summary: SSDTrain, a framework developed by researchers from NVIDIA, Google, Snowflake, UC Irvine, and UIUC, addresses the GPU memory bottleneck in Large Language Model (LLM) training by offloading activations to NVMe SSDs. The method achieved a 28% to 47% reduction in peak activation memory usage across various LLMs with negligible impact on training speed, enabling larger models and more efficient training.

title: SSDTrain: An Activation Offloading Framework to SSDs for Faster Large  Language Model Training

abstract: Efficient deployment of large language models, particularly Mixture of Experts (MoE), on resource-constrained platforms presents significant challenges, especially in terms of computational efficiency and memory utilization. The MoE architecture, renowned for its ability to increase model capacity without a proportional increase in inference cost, greatly reduces the token generation latency compared with dense models. However, the large model size makes MoE models inaccessible to individuals without high-end GPUs. In this paper, we propose a high-throughput MoE batch inference system, that significantly outperforms past work. MoE-Lightning introduces a novel CPU-GPU-I/O pipelining schedule, CGOPipe, with paged weights to achieve high resource utilization, and a performance model, HRM, based on a Hierarchical Roofline Model we introduce to help find policies with higher throughput than existing systems. MoE-Lightning can achieve up to 10.3x higher throughput than state-of-the-art offloading-enabled LLM inference systems for Mixtral 8x7B on a single T4 GPU (16GB). When the theoretical system throughput is bounded by the GPU memory, MoE-Lightning can reach the throughput upper bound with 2-3x less CPU memory, significantly increasing resource utilization. MoE-Lightning also supports efficient batch inference for much larger MoEs (e.g., Mixtral 8x22B and DBRX) on multiple low-cost GPUs (e.g., 2-4 T4).

keyInsights: MoE inference performance is bounded by a hierarchical memory system, and the Hierarchical Roofline Model (HRM) can accurately predict system behavior and guide optimization decisions for weight and KV cache placement.

Efficiently overlapping GPU computation, CPU computation, and I/O operations through a pipelining schedule like CGOPipe is crucial for overcoming performance bottlenecks in MoE models with offloaded weights.
A weight paging mechanism that prefetches expert weights based on router decisions significantly reduces pipeline bubbles and improves GPU utilization, leading to higher overall throughput.
originalProblem: Deploying large Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models on hardware with limited GPU memory (e.g., 16GB T4) is challenging due to their prohibitive memory requirements.

Existing weight offloading techniques, which store model weights in CPU memory and transfer them to GPU as needed, introduce significant I/O inefficiencies, compute-I/O imbalances, and underutilized resources.
Specific operations like Key-Value (KV) transfers and MoE Feed-Forward Network (FFN) computations become major bottlenecks, leading to long idle periods while waiting for data transfers.
results: MoE-Lightning achieves up to 10.3x higher throughput compared to FlexGen for Mixtral 8x7B on a single NVIDIA T4 GPU (16GB).
The system enables efficient batch inference for larger MoE models, including Mixtral 8x22B and DBRX, on memory-constrained GPUs like NVIDIA T4 (16GB) or L4 (24GB), which was previously infeasible.

MoE-Lightning demonstrates super-linear throughput improvements when scaling to multiple GPUs (e.g., 2 to 4 T4 GPUs), particularly for larger generation lengths, due to more efficient resource utilization.
solution: MoE-Lightning provides a high-throughput batch inference system for MoE models on memory-constrained GPUs.
It introduces CGOPipe, a CPU-GPU-I/O pipelining schedule that efficiently overlaps GPU computation, CPU computation, and data transfer events.

The system utilizes a Hierarchical Roofline Model (HRM) to accurately predict performance bottlenecks and inform optimal decisions regarding weight placement, KV cache placement, and computation scheduling.
paper_summary.summary: MoE-Lightning is a high-throughput batch inference system that enables efficient deployment of large Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models on memory-constrained GPUs. The system achieves up to 10.3x higher throughput compared to existing offloading solutions by employing a novel CPU-GPU-I/O pipelining schedule and a hierarchical performance model.

title: MoE-Lightning: High-Throughput MoE Inference on Memory-constrained GPUs

abstract:
In this paper, we explore FP8 low-bit data formats for efficient training of
large language models (LLMs). Our key insight is that most variables, such as
gradients and optimizer states, in LLM training can employ low-precision data
formats without compromising model accuracy and requiring no changes to
hyper-parameters. Specifically, we propose a new FP8 automatic mixed-precision
framework for training LLMs. This framework offers three levels of FP8
utilization to streamline mixed-precision and distributed parallel training for
LLMs. It gradually incorporates 8-bit gradients, optimizer states, and
distributed learning in an incremental manner. Experiment results show that,
during the training of GPT-175B model on H100 GPU platform, our FP8
mixed-precision training framework not only achieved a remarkable 39% reduction
in real memory usage but also ran 75% faster than the widely adopted BF16
framework (i.e., Megatron-LM), surpassing the speed of Nvidia Transformer
Engine by 37%. This largely reduces the training costs for large foundation
models. Furthermore, our FP8 mixed-precision training methodology is generic.
It can be seamlessly applied to other tasks such as LLM instruction tuning and
reinforcement learning with human feedback, offering savings in fine-tuning
expenses. Our FP8 low-precision training framework is open-sourced at
{https://github.com/Azure/MS-AMP}{aka.ms/MS.AMP}.

keyInsights: Master weights and second-order gradient moments are highly sensitive to precision and require FP16 or FP32 to maintain training stability and avoid divergence.
First-order gradient moments and the gradients themselves can be effectively represented in FP8 with appropriate tensor scaling, significantly reducing memory and communication costs.

A shared global minimum scaling factor across devices allows for efficient FP8 all-reduce communication, simplifying management of scaling factors and enabling NCCL to operate on uniformly scaled FP8 tensors.
originalProblem: The astronomical computational and memory costs of training large language models (LLMs) with traditional 16-bit or 32-bit precision methods.
The severe numerical instability challenges (underflow, overflow, quantization errors) that hinder the practical adoption of 8-bit floating-point (FP8) precision for end-to-end LLM training.

Existing FP8 implementations, such as Nvidia's Transformer Engine, only partially utilize FP8 for specific computations (e.g., GEMMs) and do not extend it to gradients, optimizer states, or collective communication, limiting overall efficiency gains.
results: Achieves comparable training loss curves and zero-shot performance to BF16 mixed-precision across various GPT-style LLMs (7B, 13B, 175B) on a wide range of downstream tasks, without hyper-parameter changes.
Delivers 28% to 39% reduction in real memory usage and 63% to 65% decrease in weight-related communication overhead compared to BF16, enabling larger batch sizes or longer sequences.
Provides a significant speed-up, up to 75% faster than BF16 and 37% faster than Nvidia Transformer Engine for GPT-175B training on H100 GPUs.
solution: A comprehensive FP8 automatic mixed-precision framework that integrates 8-bit precision across all major components of LLM training, including computation, storage, and communication.

A 'precision decoupling' strategy for the Adam optimizer, selectively storing master weights (FP16/FP32) and second-order gradient moments (FP16) in higher precision, while utilizing FP8 for first-order gradient moments and gradients.

An 'automatic scaling' technique that dynamically adjusts a global scaling factor for FP8 gradients during collective communication (e.g., all-reduce) to prevent underflow, overflow, and manage numerical stability.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Microsoft Azure and Microsoft Research developed FP8-LM, an end-to-end 8-bit floating-point (FP8) mixed-precision framework for training large language models. This framework achieves comparable accuracy to BF16 while reducing memory usage by up to 39%, communication overhead by up to 65%, and training speed by up to 75% on Nvidia H100 GPUs.

title: FP8-LM: Training FP8 Large Language Models

abstract:
We present MegaBlocks, a system for efficient Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)
training on GPUs. Our system is motivated by the limitations of current
frameworks, which restrict the dynamic routing in MoE layers to satisfy the
constraints of existing software and hardware. These formulations force a
tradeoff between model quality and hardware efficiency, as users must choose
between dropping tokens from the computation or wasting computation and memory
on padding. To address these limitations, we reformulate MoE computation in
terms of block-sparse operations and develop new block-sparse GPU kernels that
efficiently handle the dynamism present in MoEs. Our approach never drops
tokens and maps efficiently to modern hardware, enabling end-to-end training
speedups of up to 40% over MoEs trained with the state-of-the-art Tutel library
and 2.4x over DNNs trained with the highly-optimized Megatron-LM framework.

keyInsights: Reformulating MoE computation as block-sparse matrix multiplication enables direct representation of dynamic routing patterns without token dropping or padding.
Specialized GPU kernels for block-sparse operations can achieve performance comparable to highly optimized dense matrix multiplication libraries like cuBLAS.
Eliminating token dropping improves model quality and removes the need for hyperparameter tuning of the capacity factor.
originalProblem: Existing Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) implementations often drop excess tokens routed to an expert, degrading model quality.
Current MoE training requires tuning a 'capacity factor' hyperparameter, adding complexity and balancing token dropping vs. padding.
Approaches avoiding token dropping often rely on padding, leading to computational inefficiencies and wasted resources on hardware accelerators.
results: MegaBlocks achieves up to 40% faster training convergence compared to Tutel, a state-of-the-art MoE framework.

The system provides up to 2.4
× speedup over dense Transformer models trained with Megatron-LM.
MegaBlocks kernels achieve up to 98.6% of cuBLAS throughput for block-sparse matrix multiplication, especially for larger matrix sizes.
solution: MegaBlocks introduces a novel system that reformulates MoE expert computation as block-sparse matrix multiplication.
It implements specialized GPU kernels to efficiently handle the variable-sized, imbalanced routing patterns inherent in MoE models.
The approach processes all tokens without dropping, eliminating the capacity factor and avoiding padding inefficiencies.
paper_summary.summary: MegaBlocks introduces a system for training Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models by reformulating expert computation as block-sparse matrix multiplication, which processes all tokens without dropping and achieves faster training convergence with improved model quality.

title: MegaBlocks: Efficient Sparse Training with Mixture-of-Experts

abstract: Larger model sizes and longer sequence lengths have empowered the Large Language Model (LLM) to achieve outstanding performance across various domains. However, this progress brings significant storage capacity challenges for LLM pretraining. High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) is expensive and requires more advanced packaging technologies for capacity expansion, creating an urgent need for memory-efficient scheduling strategies. Yet, prior pipeline parallelism schedules have primarily focused on reducing bubble overhead, often neglecting memory efficiency and lacking compatibility with other memory-efficient strategies. Consequently, these methods struggle to meet the storage demands of storage capacity for next-generation LLM. This work presents ChronosPipe, a Chronos-aware pipeline parallelism for memory-efficient LLM pretraining. The core insight of ChronosPipe is to treat HBM as a fast but small 'cache,' optimizing and exploiting temporal locality within LLM pretraining to enhance HBM utilization. ChronosPipe introduces a pipeline scheduling strategy, Chronos-Pipe, to reduce the extrinsic overhead that disrupts the temporal locality of activations. Additionally, it leverages Chronos-Recomp and Chronos-Offload to efficiently harness the intrinsic temporal locality of activations and weights in Deep Neural Networks. Experiment results show that ChronosPipe can expand the trainable model size by 2.4x while maintaining comparable throughput, achieving 1.5x better than the 1F1B strategy combined with recomputation.
title: Enhancing Memory Efficiency in Large Language Model Training Through Chronos-aware Pipeline Parallelism

abstract:
In recent years, Large Language Models (LLMs) have exhibited remarkable
capabilities, driving advancements in real-world applications. However,
training LLMs on increasingly long input sequences imposes significant
challenges due to high GPU memory and computational demands. Existing solutions
face two key limitations: (1) memory reduction techniques, such as activation
recomputation and CPU offloading, compromise training efficiency; (2)
distributed parallelism strategies require excessive GPU resources, limiting
the scalability of input sequence length.
To address these gaps, we propose Adaptive Sequence Pipeline Parallel
Offloading (SPPO), a novel LLM training framework that optimizes memory and
computational resource efficiency for long-sequence training. SPPO introduces
adaptive offloading, leveraging sequence-aware offloading, and two-level
activation management to reduce GPU memory consumption without degrading the
training efficiency. Additionally, SPPO develops an adaptive pipeline
scheduling approach with a heuristic solver and multiplexed sequence
partitioning to improve computational resource efficiency. Experimental results
demonstrate that SPPO achieves up to 3.38x throughput improvement over
Megatron-LM and DeepSpeed, realizing efficient training of a 7B LLM with
sequence lengths of up to 4M tokens on only 128 A100 GPUs.

keyInsights: Different parts of a long sequence have varying computational requirements, making fixed offloading and pipeline scheduling policies inefficient.
Dynamically adapting offloading strategies based on sequence characteristics and maximizing overlap between CPU offloading and GPU computation significantly reduces memory footprint and overhead.
Optimizing pipeline execution through FLOPs-based partitioning and multiplexed processing reduces idle periods (pipeline bubbles) and improves hardware utilization.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) are limited by maximum sequence length (typically 4K-32K tokens), preventing processing of very long contexts like entire books or extensive documents.
Training LLMs with long sequences faces a memory bottleneck, as activation memory scales linearly with sequence length (e.g., 2.5TB for a 7B model with 1M tokens).

Existing memory reduction techniques and distributed parallelism methods often introduce significant computational overhead or require excessive GPU resources, compromising training efficiency for long sequences.
results: SPPO enables training a 7B parameter LLM with sequence lengths up to 4 million tokens using 128 A100 GPUs, which was not feasible with prior frameworks.
The framework achieves up to 3.38x throughput improvement over existing state-of-the-art frameworks like Megatron and DeepSpeed FPDT.
For a GPT-13B model with 512K tokens, SPPO provides a 1.45x speedup compared to Megatron, and a 1.28x speedup for GPT-65B with the same sequence length.
solution: Sequence Pipeline Parallel Offloading (SPPO) partitions long input sequences into multiple subsequences using a FLOPs-based strategy to ensure balanced computational loads.
SPPO employs a two-level adaptive offloading technique that dynamically determines the optimal offloading ratio and selectively keeps frequently accessed activations in GPU memory.

The framework incorporates an adaptive pipeline scheduling strategy that minimizes pipeline bubbles by using a heuristic solver to determine optimal subsequence counts and applies multiplexed sequence partitioning to interleave processing.
paper_summary.summary: SPPO introduces a framework for training large language models that enables efficient processing of sequences up to 4 million tokens, addressing the limitations of prior methods. This system achieves up to 3.38x throughput improvement over existing frameworks by integrating FLOPs-based sequence partitioning, adaptive offloading, and adaptive pipeline scheduling.

title: SPPO:Efficient Long-sequence LLM Training via Adaptive Sequence Pipeline  Parallel Offloading

abstract: Efficient and stable training of large language models (LLMs) remains a core challenge in modern machine learning systems. To address this challenge, Reparameterized Orthogonal Equivalence Training (POET), a spectrum-preserving framework that optimizes each weight matrix through orthogonal equivalence transformation, has been proposed. Although POET provides strong training stability, its original implementation incurs high memory consumption and computational overhead due to intensive matrix multiplications. To overcome these limitations, we introduce POET-X, a scalable and memory-efficient variant that performs orthogonal equivalence transformations with significantly reduced computational cost. POET-X maintains the generalization and stability benefits of POET while achieving substantial improvements in throughput and memory efficiency. In our experiments, POET-X enables the pretraining of billion-parameter LLMs on a single Nvidia H100 GPU, and in contrast, standard optimizers such as AdamW run out of memory under the same settings.

keyInsights: The inherent stability benefits of orthogonal transformations can be scaled to large language models by meticulously redesigning computations for memory and runtime efficiency.
Low-level, hardware-aware optimizations like custom CUDA kernels, batching, and kernel fusion are crucial for translating theoretical efficiency into practical performance gains on GPUs.
Employing strategies like gradient checkpointing and on-the-fly dequantization enables extreme memory efficiency, allowing billion-parameter LLMs to be trained on single high-end GPUs.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM) training is resource-intensive, often leading to instability and scalability challenges due to enormous computational and memory demands.

The original POET (Reparameterized Orthogonal Equivalence Training) offered strong theoretical stability and spectrum preservation but was impractical for LLMs due to high memory consumption and slow execution speeds.
Efficient pretraining methods for LLMs that balance stability, memory efficiency, and competitive performance are needed to democratize access to advanced model development.
results: Achieved a 3x GPU memory reduction compared to the original POET and enabled Llama-8B and Llama-13B pretraining on a single Nvidia H100 GPU.
Demonstrated an 8x runtime speedup over the original POET, with key components like permutation operators achieving up to a 20x speedup.
POET-X consistently outperformed or was competitive with state-of-the-art optimizers like AdamW, Muon, APOLLO, and GaLore in validation perplexity, especially in quantized settings.
solution: POET-X employs an input-centric reformulation of the orthogonal equivalence transformation to convert large matrix-matrix multiplications into memory-efficient matrix-vector operations.
It integrates custom CUDA operators for permutation and batch-parallel computation for block-diagonal matrix multiplications, significantly reducing runtime and memory overheads.
Optimized Cayley-Neumann Parameterization (CNP) uses reduced parameter storage and custom Triton kernels with fused computations to accelerate orthogonal matrix updates efficiently.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from The Chinese University of Hong Kong and MPI for Intelligent Systems developed POET-X, an algorithm for memory-efficient LLM training that scales orthogonal transformations. It achieves a 3x GPU memory reduction and 8x runtime speedup over its predecessor, enabling Llama-8B and Llama-13B pretraining on a single H100 GPU while maintaining competitive performance.

title: POET-X: Memory-efficient LLM Training by Scaling Orthogonal Transformation

abstract: Existing methods for training LLMs on long-sequence data, such as Tensor Parallelism and Context Parallelism, exhibit low Model FLOPs Utilization as sequence lengths and number of GPUs increase, especially when sequence lengths exceed 1M tokens. To address these challenges, we propose BurstEngine, an efficient framework designed to train LLMs on long-sequence data. BurstEngine introduces BurstAttention, an optimized distributed attention with lower communication cost than RingAttention. BurstAttention leverages topology-aware ring communication to fully utilize network bandwidth and incorporates fine-grained communication-computation overlap. Furthermore, BurstEngine introduces sequence-level selective checkpointing and fuses the language modeling head with the loss function to reduce memory cost. Additionally, BurstEngine introduces workload balance optimization for various types of attention masking. By integrating these optimizations, BurstEngine achieves a $1.2\times$ speedup with much lower memory overhead than the state-of-the-art baselines when training LLMs on extremely long sequences of over 1M tokens. We have made our code publicly available on GitHub: this https URL.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

title: BurstEngine: an Efficient Distributed Framework for Training Transformers on Extremely Long Sequences of over 1M Tokens

abstract: Large-scale model training has been a playing ground for a limited few requiring complex model refactoring and access to prohibitively expensive GPU clusters. ZeRO-Offload changes the large model training landscape by making large model training accessible to nearly everyone. It can train models with over 13 billion parameters on a single GPU, a 10x increase in size compared to popular framework such as PyTorch, and it does so without requiring any model change from the data scientists or sacrificing computational efficiency. ZeRO-Offload enables large model training by offloading data and compute to CPU. To preserve compute efficiency, it is designed to minimize the data movement to/from GPU, and reduce CPU compute time while maximizing memory savings on GPU. As a result, ZeRO-Offload can achieve 40 TFlops/GPU on a single NVIDIA V100 GPU for 10B parameter model compared to 30TF using PyTorch alone for a 1.4B parameter model, the largest that can be trained without running out of memory. ZeRO-Offload is also designed to scale on multiple-GPUs when available, offering near linear speedup on up to 128 GPUs. Additionally, it can work together with model parallelism to train models with over 70 billion parameters on a single DGX-2 box, a 4.5x increase in model size compared to using model parallelism alone. By combining compute and memory efficiency with ease-of-use, ZeRO-Offload democratizes large-scale model training making it accessible to even data scientists with access to just a single GPU.

keyInsights: A first-principles analysis reveals a unique optimal heterogeneous offload strategy that simultaneously maximizes GPU memory savings (8x reduction in model state memory) while minimizing CPU-GPU communication volume (4M bytes).

CPU memory and computation are most effectively utilized for O(M) complexity operations (like optimizer updates) and storing FP32 model states, freeing up GPU resources for O(MB) operations (forward/backward passes).

Overlapping CPU-based parameter updates with subsequent GPU computation steps via a one-step delayed parameter update strategy significantly boosts throughput without impacting convergence for small batch sizes.
originalProblem: Training billion-scale deep learning models requires immense GPU memory, often exceeding single-GPU capacity and necessitating expensive, large-scale GPU clusters.
Existing solutions for heterogeneous memory systems often focus on CNNs (activations as bottlenecks), lack efficient multi-GPU scaling, or require significant model refactoring.
The prohibitive hardware requirements limit large model training to a few well-funded organizations, hindering broader research and development.
results: Achieved training of 13 billion parameter models on a single 32GB NVIDIA V100 GPU (a 9x increase over PyTorch/ZeRO-2) and up to 70 billion parameters on a DGX-2.
Demonstrated over 40 TFLOPS/GPU for 10B parameter models on a single V100, outperforming PyTorch and L2L, due to 7x smaller CPU-GPU communication volume.
Exhibited near-perfect linear speedup in aggregate throughput on up to 128 GPUs, enabling model training from infeasible to feasible on smaller GPU counts (1-16 GPUs).
solution: A unique optimal offload strategy identifies that FP16 gradients and all FP32 optimizer states (parameters, momentum, variance) should be offloaded to CPU memory and processed by the CPU.
Integrates seamlessly with ZeRO-2 powered data parallelism and model parallelism to scale effectively from single-GPU to multi-GPU clusters.
Optimized CPU execution for Adam using SIMD, loop unrolling, OpenMP, and a one-step delayed parameter update (DPU) strategy to mitigate CPU overhead.
paper_summary.summary: Microsoft and UC Merced researchers developed ZeRO-Offload, an optimization for large language model training that enables models up to 13 billion parameters on a single GPU (NVIDIA V100 32GB) and up to 70 billion parameters on a DGX-2. This is achieved by strategically offloading FP16 gradients and FP32 optimizer states to CPU memory and computation, while maintaining high training throughput and near-linear scalability.

title: ZeRO-Offload: Democratizing Billion-Scale Model Training

abstract:
Training multi-billion to trillion-parameter language models efficiently on
GPU clusters requires leveraging multiple parallelism strategies. We present
Galvatron, a novel open-source framework (dubbed 'Optimus-Megatron' in the
implementation) that dynamically combines data parallelism, tensor model
parallelism, and pipeline parallelism to optimize training throughput. Built
atop PyTorch and integrating NVIDIA's Megatron-LM and Microsoft's DeepSpeed,
Galvatron automatically selects and adjusts parallelism strategies in real time
based on model architecture, hardware, and training dynamics. This paper
details Galvatron's key features -- automatic hybrid parallelism selection,
layer-wise and phase-wise strategy optimization, and runtime adaptation -- and
contrasts them with existing static frameworks. We describe the system's
technical stack, including its use of DeepSpeed's ZeRO and NCCL communication,
and provide an in-depth implementation overview of its core modules (profilers,
strategy selector, parallelism manager). We then illustrate how Galvatron can
be seamlessly integrated into existing training pipelines with minimal code
modifications, providing companies a plug-and-play solution for efficient
large-model training. Finally, we situate Galvatron in context with related
efforts (NVIDIA Megatron-LM, Microsoft DeepSpeed, Google GShard, Meta
FairScale, etc.), highlighting how it advances the state of the art in
distributed deep learning. References to the GitHub repository and relevant
literature are provided throughout.
title: Galvatron: Automatic Distributed Training for Large Transformer Models

abstract:
In the last three years, the largest dense deep learning models have grown over 1000x to reach hundreds of billions of parameters, while the GPU memory has only grown by 5x (16 GB to 80 GB). Therefore, the growth in model scale has been supported primarily though system innovations that allow large models to fit in the aggregate GPU memory of multiple GPUs. However, we are getting close to the GPU memory wall. It requires 800 NVIDIA V100 GPUs just to fit a trillion parameter model for training, and such clusters are simply out of reach for most data scientists. In addition, training models at that scale requires complex combinations of parallelism techniques that puts a big burden on the data scientists to refactor their model.
In this paper we present ZeRO-Infinity, a novel heterogeneous system technology that leverages GPU, CPU, and NVMe memory to allow for unprecedented model scale on limited resources without requiring model code refactoring. At the same time it achieves excellent training throughput and scalability, unencumbered by the limited CPU or NVMe bandwidth. ZeRO-Infinity can fit models with tens and even hundreds of trillions of parameters for training on current generation GPU clusters. It can be used to fine-tune trillion parameter models on a single NVIDIA DGX-2 node, making large models more accessible. In terms of training throughput and scalability, it sustains over 25 petaflops on 512 NVIDIA V100 GPUs(40% of peak), while also demonstrating super linear scalability. An open source implementation of ZeRO-Infinity is available through DeepSpeed, a deep learning optimization library that makes distributed training easy, efficient, and effective.

keyInsights: Memory-centric tiling allows processing of individual layers too large for GPU memory, enabling significantly larger hidden dimensions (e.g., up to 64K).
Bandwidth-centric partitioning efficiently distributes memory-intensive optimizer states to slower memory tiers while managing high-bandwidth parameter and gradient transfers across GPUs.
An overlap-centric design extensively hides the latency of data transfers across GPU, CPU, and NVMe memory by asynchronously prefetching and overlapping communication with computation.
originalProblem: Modern deep learning models have rapidly increased in size, often exceeding the memory capacity of even high-end GPUs.
The "GPU memory wall" makes training models with tens to hundreds of billions or trillions of parameters infeasible with traditional methods.
Existing parallelism techniques like 3D parallelism are complex and still face memory limitations for extreme-scale models, requiring over 12TB for trillion-parameter models.
results: Trained models with up to 32 trillion parameters on 512 GPUs, demonstrating a 50x increase in maximum model size compared to state-of-the-art 3D parallelism.
Achieved high training efficiency, delivering over 25 petaflops throughput on 512 GPUs and maintaining up to 49 TFlops/GPU for trillion-parameter models.
Enabled training of trillion-parameter models on a single 16-GPU DGX-2 node, making extreme-scale model research more accessible.
solution: Implements a heterogeneous memory system by combining GPU memory with larger, slower CPU RAM and NVMe storage.
Introduces an "Infinity Offload Engine" to intelligently manage and offload model components (parameters, optimizer states, gradients) across these memory tiers.
Integrates with the DeepSpeed framework, requiring minimal changes to existing PyTorch model code for ease of implementation.
paper_summary.summary: ZeRO-Infinity, developed by Microsoft, introduces a heterogeneous memory system that leverages GPU, CPU, and NVMe memory to overcome the GPU memory wall, enabling the training of deep learning models with up to 32 trillion parameters and projecting capabilities for models over 100 trillion parameters. This system makes large-scale model training more accessible by allowing trillion-parameter models to be trained on fewer GPU nodes, including single DGX-2 nodes.

title: ZeRO-Infinity: Breaking the GPU Memory Wall for Extreme Scale Deep Learning

abstract:
Parameter-Efficient Fine-tuning (PEFT) facilitates the fine-tuning of Large
Language Models (LLMs) under limited resources. However, the fine-tuning
performance with PEFT on complex, knowledge-intensive tasks is limited due to
the constrained model capacity, which originates from the limited number of
additional trainable parameters. To overcome this limitation, we introduce a
novel mechanism that fine-tunes LLMs with adapters of larger size yet
memory-efficient. This is achieved by leveraging the inherent activation
sparsity in the Feed-Forward Networks (FFNs) of LLMs and utilizing the larger
capacity of Central Processing Unit (CPU) memory compared to Graphics
Processing Unit (GPU). We store and update the parameters of larger adapters on
the CPU. Moreover, we employ a Mixture of Experts (MoE)-like architecture to
mitigate unnecessary CPU computations and reduce the communication volume
between the GPU and CPU. This is particularly beneficial over the limited
bandwidth of PCI Express (PCIe). Our method can achieve fine-tuning results
comparable to those obtained with larger memory capacities, even when operating
under more limited resources such as a 24GB memory single GPU setup, with
acceptable loss in training efficiency. Our codes are available at
https://github.com/CURRENTF/MEFT.

organization_info.name: Academy of Mathematics and Systems Science

title: MEFT: Memory-Efficient Fine-Tuning through Sparse Adapter

abstract: Nowadays, Large Language Models (LLMs) have been trained using extended context lengths to foster more creative applications. However, long context training poses great challenges considering the constraint of GPU memory. It not only leads to substantial activation memory consumption during training, but also incurs considerable memory fragmentation. To facilitate long context training, existing frameworks have adopted strategies such as recomputation and various forms of parallelisms. Nevertheless, these techniques rely on redundant computation or extensive communication, resulting in low Model FLOPS Utilization (MFU). In this paper, we propose MEMO, a novel LLM training framework designed for fine-grained activation memory management. Given the quadratic scaling of computation and linear scaling of memory with sequence lengths when using FlashAttention, we offload memory-consuming activations to CPU memory after each layer's forward pass and fetch them during the backward pass. To maximize the swapping of activations without hindering computation, and to avoid exhausting limited CPU memory, we implement a token-wise activation recomputation and swapping mechanism. Furthermore, we tackle the memory fragmentation issue by employing a bi-level Mixed Integer Programming (MIP) approach, optimizing memory reuse across transformer layers. Empirical results demonstrate that MEMO achieves an average of 1.97x and 1.80x MFU compared to Megatron-LM and DeepSpeed, respectively. This improvement is attributed to MEMO's ability to minimize memory fragmentation, reduce recomputation and intensive communication, and circumvent the delays associated with the memory reorganization process due to fragmentation. By leveraging fine-grained activation memory management, MEMO facilitates efficient training of 7B LLM with 1 million sequence length on just 8 A800 GPUs, achieving an MFU of 52.30%.
title: MEMO: Fine-grained Tensor Management For Ultra-long Context LLM Training

abstract: Training LLMs larger than the aggregated memory of multiple GPUs is increasingly necessary due to the faster growth of LLM sizes compared to GPU memory. To this end, multi-tier host memory or disk offloading techniques are proposed by state of art. Despite advanced asynchronous multi-tier read/write strategies, such offloading strategies result in significant I/O overheads in the critical path of training, resulting in slower iterations. To this end, we propose MLP-Offload, a novel multi-level, multi-path offloading engine specifically designed for optimizing LLM training on resource-constrained setups by mitigating I/O bottlenecks. We make several key observations that drive the design of MLP-Offload, such as I/O overheads during the update dominate the iteration time; I/O bandwidth of the third-level remote storage tier remains unutilized; and, contention due to concurrent offloading amplifies I/O bottlenecks. Driven by these insights, we design and implement MLP-Offload to offload the optimizer states across multiple tiers in a cache-efficient and concurrency-controlled fashion to mitigate I/O bottlenecks during the backward and update phases. Evaluations on models up to 280B parameters shows that MLP-Offload achieves 2.5$\times$ faster iterations compared to the state-of-the-art LLM training runtimes.

keyInsights: I/O bottlenecks, not computation, are the primary limitation when offloading optimizer states for large LLM pre-training on resource-constrained hardware.

A holistic approach to memory and storage management, actively utilizing the entire memory hierarchy including traditionally neglected parallel file systems, is crucial for scaling LLM training efficiently.

System-level optimizations, such as intelligent data placement, concurrency control, and optimized data conversion strategies, can provide substantial performance gains beyond algorithmic or parallelism improvements.
originalProblem: The exponential growth of Large Language Models (LLMs) in parameters and data size far outpaces GPU memory capacity, leading to a "GPU memory wall" that makes training prohibitively expensive or impossible.
Existing offloading solutions, especially to NVMe SSDs, create significant I/O bottlenecks, where the optimizer state update phase can be 99% dominated by I/O operations.

High-performance computing (HPC) systems feature underutilized remote storage (e.g., parallel file systems) with substantial bandwidth that current LLM training frameworks do not actively leverage for offloading.
results: MLP-Offload achieves up to 2.5 times faster training iterations for LLMs ranging from 40B to 280B parameters compared to DeepSpeed ZeRO-3.
It significantly accelerates the backward pass by 13.5 times and the update phase by 2.3 times, while boosting effective I/O throughput to 8.5 GB/s, a 2.6 times increase over the baseline.

The framework provides a 2 times improvement in cost-effectiveness relative to DeepSpeed ZeRO-3 when comparing to GPU-only training, and delivers at least a 40% speedup even with gradient accumulation.
solution: MLP-Offload implements a unified multi-level, multi-path asynchronous offloading engine using virtual tiers to load-balance optimizer states across GPU, host DRAM, local NVMe, and remote parallel file systems based on a performance model.
It introduces optimized concurrency control for multi-path I/O, granting exclusive access to storage paths for individual workers to minimize contention and maximize effective bandwidth.

The engine employs cache-friendly ordering of model subgroup processing to reduce host memory thrashing and utilizes delayed in-place mixed-precision gradient conversion to significantly reduce data transferred during I/O.
paper_summary.summary: MLP-Offload, developed by researchers at Argonne National Laboratory and Rochester Institute of Technology, introduces a multi-level, multi-path offloading engine that mitigates severe I/O bottlenecks in large language model pre-training. This system leverages the entire memory hierarchy to achieve up to 2.5 times faster training iterations for models up to 280 billion parameters, effectively breaking the GPU memory wall.

title: MLP-Offload: Multi-Level, Multi-Path Offloading for LLM Pre-training to Break the GPU Memory Wall

abstract: Pipeline parallelism (PP) is widely used for training large language models (LLMs), yet its scalability is often constrained by high activation memory consumption as the number of in-flight microbatches grows with the degree of PP. In this paper, we focus on addressing this challenge by leveraging the under-explored memory offload strategy in PP. With empirical study, we discover that in the majority of standard configurations, at least half, and potentially all, of the activations can be offloaded with negligible overhead. In the cases where full overload is not possible, we introduce a novel selective offload strategy that decreases peak activation memory in a better-than-linear manner. Furthermore, we integrate memory offload with other techniques to jointly consider overall throughput and memory limitation. Our experiments proves that the per-device activation memory effectively reduces with the total number of stages, making PP a stronger alternative than TP, offering up to a 19\% acceleration with even lower memory consumption. The implementation is open-sourced at \href{this https URL}{this url}.
title: PipeOffload: Improving Scalability of Pipeline Parallelism with Memory Optimization

abstract: Model parallelism has become a necessity for training modern large-scale deep language models. In this work, we identify a new and orthogonal dimension from existing model parallel approaches: it is possible to perform pipeline parallelism within a single training sequence for Transformer-based language models thanks to its autoregressive property. This enables a more fine-grained pipeline compared with previous work. With this key idea, we design TeraPipe, a high-performance token-level pipeline parallel algorithm for synchronous model-parallel training of Transformer-based language models. We develop a novel dynamic programming-based algorithm to calculate the optimal pipelining execution scheme given a specific model and cluster configuration. We show that TeraPipe can speed up the training by 5.0x for the largest GPT-3 model with 175 billion parameters on an AWS cluster with 48 p3.16xlarge instances compared with state-of-the-art model-parallel methods. The code for reproduction can be found at this https URL
title: TeraPipe: Token-Level Pipeline Parallelism for Training Large-Scale Language Models

abstract: It is extremely memory-hungry to train Large Language Models (LLM). To solve this problem, existing work exploits the combination of CPU and GPU for the training process, such as ZeRO-Offload. Such a technique largely democratizes billion-scale model training, making it possible to train with few consumer graphics cards. However, based on our observation, existing frameworks often provide coarse-grained memory management and require experienced experts in configuration tuning, leading to suboptimal hardware utilization and performance. This paper proposes ProTrain, a novel training system that intelligently balances memory usage and performance by coordinating memory, computation, and IO. ProTrain achieves adaptive memory management through Chunk-Based Model State Management and Block-Wise Activation Management, guided by a Memory-Aware Runtime Profiler without user intervention. ProTrain does not change the training algorithm and thus does not compromise accuracy. Experiments show that ProTrain improves training throughput by 1.43$\times$ to 2.71$\times$ compared to the SOTA training systems.

keyInsights: Intelligently overlapping CPU-based parameter updates with GPU backward computations can effectively hide CPU offloading overhead, significantly improving overall training throughput.
Adaptive, fine-grained memory management at the chunk level for model states and block level for activations optimizes heterogeneous memory utilization better than coarse-grained approaches.
Precise runtime and peak memory usage estimation, even for very large models, is crucial for automatically identifying optimal configurations without manual tuning.
originalProblem: Training Large Language Models (LLMs) faces severe memory constraints due to the immense size of model states and activations, leading to bottlenecks.
Existing distributed training frameworks offer coarse-grained memory management, providing limited options for utilizing heterogeneous memory resources optimally.
Efficient LLM training often requires extensive manual configuration and expertise to tune numerous parameters, posing a significant barrier for users.
results: ProTrain enabled training of significantly larger models, supporting up to 30 billion parameters on RTX 3090 GPUs and 70 billion parameters on A100 GPUs.
The framework achieved an average training throughput improvement of 1.77 to 2.71 times on RTX 3090s and 1.85 to 2.25 times on A100s compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

ProTrain's adaptive memory management led to consistently lower runtimes than fixed configurations, with its runtime and peak memory usage estimators demonstrating high accuracy (less than 5% and 10% error, respectively).
solution: ProTrain employs a chunk-based dual-chunk system for model states, utilizing persistent chunks on GPU for direct updates and non-persistent chunks on CPU with concurrent updates to hide overhead.
It introduces block-wise activation management, selectively applying swapping or gradient checkpointing at the transformer block level to optimize memory usage during forward and backward passes.

A Memory-Aware Runtime Profiler accurately profiles memory requirements and execution times, feeding data to an Adaptive Memory Management module that automatically searches for optimal configurations.
paper_summary.summary: ProTrain introduces an adaptive memory management system for large language models, intelligently coordinating memory, computation, and I/O to overcome memory bottlenecks and manual configuration complexities. This framework supports training models up to 70 billion parameters on a single A100 GPU and delivers up to 2.71 times higher average training throughput compared to existing methods.

title: ProTrain: Efficient LLM Training via Memory-Aware Techniques

abstract:
Transformer-based large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional
capabilities in sequence modeling and text generation, with improvements
scaling proportionally with model size. However, the limitations of GPU memory
have restricted LLM training accessibility for many researchers. Existing
heterogeneous training methods significantly expand the scale of trainable
models but introduce substantial communication overheads and CPU workloads. In
this work, we propose AutoHete, an automatic and efficient heterogeneous
training system compatible with both single-GPU and multi-GPU environments.
AutoHete dynamically adjusts activation checkpointing, parameter offloading,
and optimizer offloading based on the specific hardware configuration and LLM
training needs. Additionally, we design a priority-based scheduling mechanism
that maximizes the overlap between operations across training iterations,
enhancing throughput. Compared to state-of-the-art heterogeneous training
systems, AutoHete delivers a 1.32x~1.91x throughput improvement across various
model sizes and training configurations.

organization_info.name: Agency for Science, Technology and Research

keyInsights: Optimal heterogeneous training requires a holistic approach that dynamically combines multiple memory-saving techniques like activation checkpointing, parameter offloading, and optimizer offloading, instead of relying on a single method.

Overlapping operations across different training iterations, particularly by prioritizing gradient offloading and optimizer updates for earlier layers, significantly reduces GPU idle time and improves throughput.
An automatic optimization framework, leveraging a detailed cost model and an Integer Linear Program solver, can effectively determine optimal heterogeneous training strategies without manual tuning.
originalProblem: Training large language models (LLMs) is severely constrained by the "GPU memory wall," as the combined requirements for model parameters, gradients, optimizer states, and activations often exceed available GPU memory capacity.
Existing solutions like distributed training or memory-saving techniques often introduce trade-offs in efficiency, hardware costs, or implementation complexity.
The high memory demands limit LLM development primarily to organizations with significant computational resources, hindering broader innovation.
results: AutoHete achieves 1.32x to 1.91x higher throughput in single-GPU settings compared to state-of-the-art heterogeneous training systems like ZeRO-Offload, PatrickStar, and StrongHold.
The system maintains its performance advantages in multi-GPU distributed settings and consistently performs well across various batch sizes.
